coredata.dc:description: In 2020, Rainbow trout production reached 959,600 tons, representing 4 % of the global trout output. However, microbial contamination and lipid and protein oxidation spoil around 15 % of total aquatic products. Traditional food preservatives, used for their antibacterial or antioxidant properties, often fall short due to the disparity between the radical-scavenging ability of antioxidants and the ROS-generating capability of antimicrobial agents. This study developed bifunctional xylitol-based carbon dots (xβ-CDs) using 2-hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin (2-HP-β-CD), which exhibited both super antioxidant and high antibacterial activities. The incorporation of xylitol into xβ-CDs substantially enhanced their functional performance. The xβ-CDs displaying remarkable inhibition of DPPH (1-Diphenyl-2-trinitrophenylhydrazine), ABTS (2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate), and ·OH radicals (96.31 %, 99.74 %, and 99.72 %, respectively), rivaling the antioxidant capacity of vitamin C (VC). The xβ-CDs also effectively combated spoilage bacteria by compromising the cell wall and membrane integrity, reducing total protein, AKP, and ATP enzyme levels by 51.25 %, 70.08 %, and 57.80 %, respectively. Moreover, storage tests revealed that xβ-CDs extended the shelf life of Rainbow trout fillets by 3 days. These results indicate that xylitol/2-hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin carbon dots offer a promising approach to preventing bacterial infections, protein oxidation, and lipid oxidation in rainbow trout.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In 2020, Rainbow trout production reached 959,600 tons, representing 4 % of the global trout output. However, microbial contamination and lipid and protein oxidation spoil around 15 % of total aquatic products. Traditional food preservatives, used for their antibacterial or antioxidant properties, often fall short due to the disparity between the radical-scavenging ability of antioxidants and the ROS-generating capability of antimicrobial agents. This study developed bifunctional xylitol-based carbon dots (xβ-CDs) using 2-hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin (2-HP-β-CD), which exhibited both super antioxidant and high antibacterial activities. The incorporation of xylitol into xβ-CDs substantially enhanced their functional performance. The xβ-CDs displaying remarkable inhibition of DPPH (1-Diphenyl-2-trinitrophenylhydrazine), ABTS (2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate), and ·OH radicals (96.31 %, 99.74 %, and 99.72 %, respectively), rivaling the antioxidant capacity of vitamin C (VC). The xβ-CDs also effectively combated spoilage bacteria by compromising the cell wall and membrane integrity, reducing total protein, AKP, and ATP enzyme levels by 51.25 %, 70.08 %, and 57.80 %, respectively. Moreover, storage tests revealed that xβ-CDs extended the shelf life of Rainbow trout fillets by 3 days. These results indicate that xylitol/2-hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin carbon dots offer a promising approach to preventing bacterial infections, protein oxidation, and lipid oxidation in rainbow trout.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rainbow trout is known for its exceptional nutritional value, with its meat being rich in protein and low in carbohydrates, which offers substantial health benefits. Moreover, fish protein is considered among the most valuable animal proteins. Globally, Oncorhynchus mykiss is among the most extensively farmed carnivorous cold-water fish species and is promoted as a high-quality fish (Abidin et al., 2022; Xie & Liu, 2023). Rainbow trout is highly consumed, primarily in the fresh form (fish fillets). However, the aquatic product industry faces challenges because of its perishable nature, which leads to enormous waste generation (Wu et al., 2021). Fat and protein oxidation and microbial infection are primary factors contributing to the spoilage of Rainbow trout. Of them, fat oxidation often occurs when oxygen interacts with the double bonds present in fatty acids. The rapid degradation of fish meat caused by unsaturated lipid peroxidation is a common indicator of fat oxidation (Nie et al., 2022). The emergence of carbonyl groups and mercaptan depletion generally serve as measures reflective of the overall extent of protein oxidation (Zhang et al., 2024). Microbial spoilage degrades fresh fish meat and reduces its quality. Bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms primarily drive this degradation, which metabolizes nutrients within the fish, thereby fostering microbial growth and proliferation and consequently leading to spoilage (Tavares et al., 2021). The preservatives currently used in Rainbow trout fillets to prevent spoilage primarily comprise various food additives, such as organic acids and enzyme preparations (Mei et al., 2019). However, these preservatives face numerous challenges, including drug resistance (Wu et al., 2022), environmental pollution (Long et al., 2021), and dosage limitations (Cui et al., 2024). Consequently, developing novel preservatives is imperative.

At present, some nanomaterials have been widely used for preserving aquatic products, which have achieved notable effectiveness (Çiçek & Özoğul, 2023; Qiu et al., 2022). Of them, carbon dots (CDs) are considered excellent raw materials for producing novel aquatic preservatives, as they have a simple preparation, low cost, small size, abundant surface functional groups, no or low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and high antibacterial and antioxidant activities (Cui et al., 2024; Cui et al., 2023). Functioning as antioxidants, CDs are pivotal players in biological systems. Because of their remarkable stability, CDs have exceptional free radical scavenging capabilities (Christensen et al., 2011). They can effectively curtail oxidative stress reactions and thus mitigate cellular and tissue oxidative damage, thereby contributing to the maintenance of the oxidative equilibrium within biological systems (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Additionally, CDs exert bactericidal effects through diverse mechanisms. They can interact with microbial cells, causing physical harm or inducing oxidative stress reactions, thereby impeding microbial growth and replication. Moreover, CDs may disrupt microbial metabolic processes or generate active substances through photocatalytic activity, which results in bactericidal effects on microorganisms (Jiang et al., 2024; Zhao, Liu, et al., 2023). Some studies have also used CDs for preserving aquatic products (Cui et al., 2023; Zhao, Zhang, et al., 2023), indicating their potential as novel preservatives.

Xylitol, a naturally occurring five-carbon sugar alcohol (Wang, Liu, et al., 2024), represents a highly valuable bioproduct generated via microbial fermentation, offering substantial potential across a diverse array of applications. Its low caloric value, polyhydroxy structure, and renewable origin from plant-derived fibers have facilitated its extensive use in industries such as food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, with particular prominence in low-carbohydrate and functional formulations. Empirical studies have highlighted its pronounced antibacterial and antioxidant activities, positioning xylitol as a preferred sugar substitute in the food sector (Ahuja et al., 2022). Moreover, its distinctive chemical profile as a sugar alcohol makes it an excellent candidate for advanced functionalization strategies, such as cyclodextrin modification, which may further augment its bioactivity and expand its applicability. As a result, xylitol ranks among the top 12 global bioproducts, underscoring its critical role in food, nutrition, and pharmaceutical sectors while revealing significant market and research opportunities (Shazali et al., 2018). Xylitol, as a crucial precursor in the synthesis of carbon dots, significantly influences the morphology and surface functionalization of these materials due to its distinctive molecular structure and rich hydroxyl groups. This structural configuration is anticipated to endow carbon dots with superior antibacterial and antioxidant properties, thereby augmenting their potential applications in food preservation. Nonetheless, the body of research on the utilization of xylitol in carbon dot synthesis and its associated functionalities is currently limited. Therefore, there is a pressing need for comprehensive studies to elucidate its specific role and advantages within the realm of carbon dot technology. Based on the polyol structure of xylitol and the inclusion properties of 2-HP-β-CD, we hypothesize that the carbon dots synthesized from these two materials will exhibit exceptional antibacterial and antioxidant effects. The surface functionalization of these carbon dots may enhance their interactions with microorganisms, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth and improving their potential application in food preservation. Furthermore, the antioxidant properties of the carbon dots are expected to mitigate the oxidation processes in food, thereby extending shelf life. We further speculate that this novel carbon dot formulation will effectively protect the bioactive components in food, preserving their nutritional value, thus providing a safe and efficient solution for food preservation.

Traditional synthesized CDs exhibited antioxidant properties by scavenging free radicals, thereby protecting cells from oxidative damage (Zhou et al., 2024). Conversely, many antibacterial mechanisms relied on generating free radicals, such as reactive oxygen species (ROS), to kill bacteria (Cui et al., 2023). This inherent contradiction significantly restricts the ability to combine both functionalities effectively. Herein, we are preparing to develop bifunctional CDs s with super antioxidant and high antibacterial abilities. The synthesized CDs is validated through various characterization techniques. The antioxidant mechanism of CDs was elucidated by investigating their activities. The CDs are prepared to elucidate their antibacterial effects, specifically by investigating the antibacterial mechanisms against various bacteria responsible for the spoilage of aquatic products. Additionally, we are preparing to explore the influence of CDs on the quality of Rainbow trout fish fillets during storage. The present study a novel strategy for preventing bacterial infections, protein oxidation, and lipid oxidation in food preservation.

Xylitol (AR, 98 %) and 2-HP-β-CD (AR, 97 %, degree of substitution is 3.6) were Procured from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Fluorescein diacetate and PBS buffer were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Potassium persulfate (AR, 99.5 %) was Procured from MackLin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd.

The xβ-CDs was preparated by hydrothermal method (Ezati et al., 2022). Specifically, 0.5 g of xylitol and 0.5 g of 2-HP-β-CD were introduced into 20.0 mL of deionized water and then moved into a high-pressure autoclave lined with a 50.0 mL Teflon container. Heating the mixture at 260℃ for 5 h, thereafter filtration removed most of the insoluble impurities. Subsequently, the solution underwent purification for approximately 4 h using a dialysis bag (2000 Da). Freeze-drying was performed on the resulting solution to obtain xβ-CDs.

The Scavenging of hydroxyl radicals was conducted according to the method described by Kong et al (Kong et al., 2022). A 5.0 mg/mL FeSO4 solution was prepared. Then, 100.0 μL FeSO4 solution were mixed with 10.0 μL of DMPO (5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, pure DMPO) and 80.0 μL of deionized water. Subsequently, 10.0 μL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide was added and reacted for 5 min. The data was collected as the control group data. The 80.0 μL of deionized water was replaced with xβ-CDs solution at same volume were used as experiment group.

The Scavenging of singlet oxygen was done according to the literature with some modifications (Liu et al., 2021). A 2.0 mg/mL aqueous solution of TiO2 nanometer powder (Aladdin) was prepared. Then, a 50.0 μL aqueous solution of TiO2 nanometer solution was combined with 50.0 μL of a 100.0 mM TEMP(4-Amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine) solution and 100.0 μL of deionized water, respectively. Then, the samples were tested using capillary filling. After the test, data was collected as the control group data after exposing the samples to 300 W xenon lamp light for 10 min. The samples were prepared as 1.0 mg/mL solutions, and in the sample test groups, 100.0 μL of deionized water was replaced with 100.0 μL of xβ-CDs solution.

The Scavenging of hydrogen peroxide was done according to the literature with some modifications (He et al., 2014). A CTPO (3-Carbamoyl-2,2,5,5-Tetramethyl-3-Pyrrolin-1-Yloxy) solution with a volume of 500.0 μL and a concentration of 100.0 mM was prepared, along with a 400.0 μL solution of H2O2 with a concentration of 20.0 mM. Then, a 100.0 μL of xβ-CDs solution at a concentration of 1 mg/mL was added to the prepared mixture, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min to remove oxygen. experimental group data was collected. A 100.0 μL of xβ-CDs solution was replaced with 100.0 μL of deionized water for control group data.

The MIC was done according to the literature with some modifications (Cui et al., 2024). Various bacteria were activated, and 20.0 μL of bacterial suspension was taken and inoculated into 10.0 mL of broth medium, then cultured in a shaking incubator for 24 h (6000 rpm). Subsequently, the activated bacterial suspension was subcultured in a shaking incubator for 12 h. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Serratia marcescens (S.marcescens) were cultured at 37 °C, while other strains were cultured at 28 °C. Following the incubation period, the OD595 nm (OD value at 595 nm) of each bacterial suspension was quantified by a microplate reader, with a bacterial concentration controlled at 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL for subsequent experiments. Mixing xβ-CDs at different concentrations with each bacterial suspension. Then, 100.0 μL of bacterial suspension was plated onto agar medium and allowed to grow upside down in a constant temperature and humidity incubator for 12 h to observe colony growth. Different strains were cultured under their respective optimal growth conditions. The minimum inhibitory concentration of xβ-CDs against different strains was determined by observing the colony distribution on the agar plate.

The CLSM was investigated as previously described by Liu et al (Liu, Gu, et al., 2022). The bacterial in the logarithmic growth phase were centrifuged and resuspended in aseptic 0.85 % NaCl solution (bacterial suspension concentration of 1 × 107 CFU/mL). The suspended cells were treated with carbon dots at different concentrations, with a control group consisting of bacterial suspension without xβ-CDs. The suspensions were cultured at 28 °C for 4 h, then the bacterial suspension was washed twice with 0.85 % NaCl solution. Subsequently, 1.5 μL of green fluorescent dye acridine orange (AO) and 1.5 μL of red fluorescent dye propidium iodide (PI) were added to 1.0 mL of bacterial suspension. After incubating in darkness at standard room temperature for 15 min, the bacterial suspension was washed twice with 0.85 % NaCl solution. Finally, the bacterial samples were observed and recorded using laser scanning confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of bacterial morphological features using scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM was done according to the literature with some modifications (Du et al., 2023). The logarithmic phase-grown Hafnia alvei bacterial (1.0 × 107 CFU/mL) were centrifuged at 1250g for 10 min, and the bacterial pellets were collected after washing three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS). Then, the resuspended bacterial were mixed with xβ-CDs solution at different concentrations. A control test was performed using PBS mixed with xβ-CDs solution. After incubating on a shaker for 6 h, the bacterial were centrifuged and washed thrice with PBS. Then, the bacterial were fixed on zinc sheets (5 mm × 5 mm) using 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 4 h. Following this, a gradient elution with ethanol concentrations of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 % was performed, with each concentration incubated for 15 min. After air-drying, the zinc sheets were gold-coated and analyzed under a SEM for cell morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fresh-keeping effect of xβ-CDs on Rainbow trout fillets
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh Rainbow trout fillets were submerged in different treatment solutions for 10 min and then stored in sterile fresh containers. The Rainbow trout fillets were separated into five groups: (1) control group; (2) 2-HP-β-CD processing group; (3) xylitol processing group; (4) sodium dehydroacetate processing group; (5) xβ-CDs processing group. All samples were preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C. After a certain storage period, on days 0, 3, 6, 9 and 12, their sliced tissue, total viable counts (TVC), pH, total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), and thiobarbituric acid (TBA) values were determined. On days 0, 6, and 12, the Rainbow trout samples were stained with eosin and hematoxylin (Feng et al., 2017; Nie et al., 2022).
The experimental procedures are detailed in the Supplementary Information.

All test parameters were applied to three parallel samples. The data were analyzed by Graphpad Pism 9.5 and Origin 2021 software. A significance level of P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of xylitol, 2-HP-β-CD and xβ-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This study conducted a detailed structural analysis of xylitol and 2-HP-β-CD using 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectrum of xylitol (Fig. S1A) showed proton signals primarily between 3.5 and 4.0 ppm (Ohno et al., 2024), with a distinct doublet at 3.63–3.64 ppm corresponding to methylene groups (–CH2OH). Other methyl protons appeared at 3.47–3.54 ppm (Zamora et al., 2005), reflecting molecular symmetry and environmental factors. Weak spin–spin coupling and hydrogen bonding interactions contributed to the observed multiplet features, validating xylitol's molecular composition. For 2-HP-β-CD (Fig. S1B), the 1H NMR spectrum revealed proton signals between 1 and 5 ppm, indicating distinct proton environments (Kalydi et al., 2024). Strong signals at 4.66–5.10 ppm corresponded to C1 protons (H1) at the glycosidic bond, while signals between 3.78–3.88 ppm were assigned to protons at C2, C3, and C5 positions, linked to hydroxyl groups. The signal at 1.01 ppm represented terminal methyl protons (–CH3) of the hydroxypropyl substituent, characteristic of typical methyl groups (Adhikari et al., 2018).

In xylitol (Fig. S1C), the carbon signals clustered between 60–80 ppm, reflecting the molecule's symmetry and the presence of hydroxyl groups. Notably, the signal at 71.93 ppm was attributed to a primary carbon (C5) linked to a hydroxyl group, while the 70.80 ppm signal corresponded to a methylene carbon (–CH2OH). The signal at 62.65 ppm represented a methyl carbon, validating the molecular composition of xylitol. For 2-HP-β-CD (Fig. S1D), the 13C NMR spectrum showed significant shifts between 0–220 ppm. The signals at 101.50 ppm and 99.45 ppm corresponded to C1 carbon atoms in the glycosidic bonds of the β-cyclodextrin backbone, while the 80.98 ppm shift was associated with the C2 carbon in the 2-hydroxypropyl substituent. Additional signals at 72–73 ppm were linked to C2, C3, and C5 carbons within the cyclic structure, indicating minimal disruption by the hydroxypropyl group. The methyl carbon at the terminal end of the hydroxypropyl substituent appeared at 18.29 ppm (Kalydi et al., 2024), confirming successful structural modification while preserving the integrity of the cyclodextrin framework.

Through FTIR spectroscopy, the surface activity groups of xβ-CDs were investigated (Fig. S2). The FT-IR spectrum of xylitol exhibited a broad and intense absorption peak in the range of 3200–3600 cm−1, which is attributed to the O–H stretching vibrations, consistent with the presence of multiple hydroxyl groups in its molecular structure. Absorption peaks in the 2800–3000 cm−1 region correspond to C–H stretching vibrations, while peaks in the 1000–1200 cm−1 region are associated with C–O stretching vibrations, reflecting the characteristic alcohol groups in xylitol.

For 2-HP-β-CD, the FT-IR spectrum revealed abundant surface functional groups. The broad peak at 3330.00 cm−1 is also attributed to O–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups (Watanabe et al., 2020). Additionally, the peak at 2924.03 cm−1 corresponds to C–H stretching (Kumar et al., 2024), the peak at 1639.99 cm−1 is related to O–H stretching (Nasrabadi et al., 2013), and the peak at 1330.68 cm−1 is associated with the O–H and C–OH group. Absorption peaks in the 1000–1100 cm−1 region correspond to C–OH stretching vibrations, while peaks in the 1000–1200 cm−1 region are associated with C–O–C stretching vibrations. These results suggest that 2-HP-β-CD possesses a variety of surface functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which impart excellent water solubility (Lan et al., 2015), antioxidant properties (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023), and antibacterial activity (Du et al., 2023). These functional groups enable xβ-CDs to reduce oxidative stress (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023) and inhibit microbial growth (Yang et al., 2016) by altering cell or microbial structure and function.

Through the hydrothermal method, this study synthesized xβ-CDs by using xylitol and 2-HP-β-CD as precursors. The morphology of xβ-CDs was analyzed via TEM (Fig. 1 A). The xβ-CDs were well-crystallized spheres with uniform distribution, and their average particle size was 1.146 ± 0.996 nm (n = 95) (Zhang et al., 2023). The High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) examination of xβ-CDs unveiled distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm, which corresponded to the (100) lattice plane of graphite (Garcia-Millan et al., 2022).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern revealed a core position around 2θ = 20.31682°, which corresponded to the graphite carbon (002) plane. This indicates that the interlayer spacing of the graphitic-like structure is larger than that of typical graphite, suggesting looser or less ordered stacking with the presence of more defects and higher disorder (Mintz et al., 2021). These findings imply that xβ-CDs exhibit certain crystalline characteristics while predominantly displaying amorphous features, making it challenging to classify them into a single conventional crystalline phase (Fig. 1B). Further XRD analysis confirms that the carbon atoms in xβ-CDs are arranged with a high degree of disorder, reflecting a complex structure characterized by local order and overall disorder (Wei et al., 2020).

The XPS spectrum depicted that xβ-CDs only contained carbon (C) and oxygen (O), with respective contents of 72.31 % and 27.69 % (Fig. 1C). The high-resolution C1s spectrum of xβ-CDs resolved into five peaks, with peak positions at 284.45, 284.93, 286.07, 287.47 and 288.96 eV, which aligned with C–C, C–O–C, C–O, O–C=O and C–H/C=O bonds, separately (Fig. 1D) (Dewangan et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2024; Liu, Yin, et al., 2022; Mao et al., 2024; Shibu et al., 2022). The high-resolution O1s spectrum of xβ-CDs resolved into four peaks, with peak positions at 532.91, 532.80, 532.56, and 532.26 eV, which aligned with C=O, –COOH, C–O, and C–O–C/O–H bonds, separately (Fig. 1E) (Gu et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). According to the XPS results, the surface of xβ-CDs was rich in hydrophilic moieties. The zeta potential of xβ-CDs was related to the surface-associated functional groups. The zeta potential of the carbon dots under neutral conditions was −10.3 Mv (Fig. 1F). This result indicated that the xβ-CDs surface contained a significant amount of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which resulted in the xβ-CDs carrying a negative charge. Bacterial surfaces are usually negatively charged, which indicates that carrying a negative charge was favorable for the antibacterial effects of xβ-CDs (Zhu et al., 2019).

Through FTIR spectroscopy, the surface activity groups of xβ-CDs were investigated (Fig. 1G). A broad absorption peak at 3300.69 cm−1 was attributable to vibrational stretching motion of O–H bonds. Infrared vibration peaks at 2939, 1641, 1408, 1223, and 1085 cm−1 correlated with C–H, C=O, COOH, C–O, and C–O–C bonds, separately (Man et al., 2021; Ullal et al., 2023; Wang, Xu, et al., 2019; Ali et al., 2021; Wang, Liu, et al., 2019). The peak at 1043.44 cm−1 was associated with the C–O bond (Ullal et al., 2023). According to the infrared spectroscopy results, abundant functional groups were present on the xβ-CDs surface, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This feature imparted excellent water solubility (Mohammed et al., 2023), antioxidant properties (Roy et al., 2022), and antibacterial activity (Liu, Gu, et al., 2022) to the carbon dots. By altering cell or microbial structure and function, xβ-CDs reduce oxidative stress and microbial growth.

Xβ-CDs exhibited an ultraviolet absorption peak at 248 nm, associated with the n-π* transition of the C=O bond (Fig. 1H) (Veeresh et al., 2021). At an optimal excitation wavelength of 374 nm, the emission wavelength was 457 nm. Fig. 1I illustrates the emission spectra of xβ-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. It exhibits a redshift of the fluorescence emission peak and a variation in the fluorescence intensity, thereby indicating excitation-dependence, which possibly was due to the complex structure and non-uniform particle size of xβ-CDs (Ma et al., 2019). Based on the structure analysis results, we speculated that xβ-CDs can be used as competitive nanomaterials for combating bacterial contamination and alleviating oxidative damage.

The DPPH radical assay is among the mature, reliable, and simple methods for estimating antioxidant activity. The DPPH molecule itself is a free radical and contains unpaired electrons in its structure, which give the molecule a strong ultraviolet absorption capability. On coming in contact with an antioxidant, DPPH captures hydrogen atoms from the antioxidant, forming a stable molecule and reducing its absorption capacity. The DPPH solution exhibits an absorption peak at 517 nm. Because of the reaction between antioxidants and DPPH, the DPPH molecule loses its unpaired electrons, which causes the ultraviolet absorption peak to shift to shorter wavelengths, thereby changing from purple to pale yellow. The absorbance at 517 nm is used to assess the antioxidant capacity of a substance. VC is a commercial antioxidant that is preferred for comparing the antioxidant efficiency. The DPPH radical scavenging rates of xβ-CDs and VC are presented in Fig. 2 A. The DPPH scavenging rate increased continuously as the xβ-CDs concentration increased (0–0.5 mg/mL). Once the concentration exceeded 0.5 mg/mL, the DPPH radical scavenging rate exceeded 90 % and tended to stabilize, similar to that of VC. The ABTS molecule itself is also a free radical. In its oxidized state, the ABTS molecule contains unpaired electrons in its structure, which give the molecule a strong absorbance capability. When ABTS comes in contact with antioxidants, it accepts electrons from the antioxidants and transforms into its reduced form, thereby losing its free radical nature. Like DPPH, the reduced form of ABTS displays different absorption spectra characteristics. Hence, the color of the solution changes during reduction. ABTS reacts with potassium persulfate to produce the blue-green ABTS free radical, which is then reduced by the added antioxidants, leading to a lighter color of the system. The antioxidant capacity of substances is generally evaluated by measuring the absorbance at 734 nm. When the xβ-CDs concentration ranged from 0 to 0.5 mg/mL, the ABTS free radical scavenging rate increased with an increase in concentration (Fig. 2B). Of note, when the concentration reached 0.5 mg/mL, the free radical scavenging rate reached 99.74 %, similar to that of VC. The experimental results indicate that xβ-CDs have a strong ABTS free radical scavenging ability. Because of their excellent hydrophilicity, carbon dots exhibit a higher affinity for the ABTS water-based solution than with the ethanol–DPPH solution. This increased interaction is likely because of the affinity of the hydrophilic carbon dots for the water-based environment of the ABTS solution, whereas the ethanol–based DPPH solution is less compatible with their hydrophilic nature. Consequently, the reactivity and efficiency of carbon dots are augmented in the ABTS assay, which renders it a more suitable medium for such interactions (Ezati et al., 2022). The antioxidant activity of carbon dots is due to their surface oxygenation functional groups, which can neutralize ABTS and DPPH radicals by transferring H+. This occurs through a combination of hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and radical adduct generation by the carbon dots (Sul et al., 2023). The synthesized carbon dots exhibit powerful antioxidant properties, attributed to the presence of hydroxyl groups.

Hydroxyl radicals are another highly oxidizing species that can impair cell membranes and DNA, thereby resulting in substantial oxidative stress. In the present study, the xβ-CDs scavenging ability against hydroxyl radicals was investigated using the salicylic acid method. Salicylic acid reacts with hydroxyl radicals generated by Fe3+-induced H2O2, with the absorbance at 510 nm being negatively correlated with antioxidant activity (Dong et al., 2024). xβ-CDs could eliminate hydroxyl radicals, and the scavenging effect was closely related to their concentration (Fig. 2C). As the xβ-CDs concentration increased, the scavenging effect improved. When the xβ-CDs concentration ranged from 0 to 2.0 mg/mL, the hydroxyl radical scavenging rate increased with an increasing concentration, reaching 97.72 % at 2.0 mg/mL, similar to that of VC. The antioxidant activity and free radical scavenging ability of xβ-CDs were further evaluated using an acidic KMnO4 solution. KMnO4 exists in the molecular form and is unstable and prone to oxygen release, which renders it a potent oxidizing agent. When antioxidants are present, MnO4 − is reduced to MnO4 2− under acidic conditions, which induces a change in color from purple to colorless (Zhao, Dufresne, et al., 2023). The xβ-CDs exhibited a concentration-dependent increase in the KMnO4 scavenging ability (Fig. 2D). Specifically, in the 0–0.5 mg/mL concentration range, the KMnO4 scavenging rate increased as the concentration increased, reaching 83.91 % at 0.5 mg/mL xβ-CDs. To further comprehend the oxidative stability of xβ-CDs, we examined the behavior of linoleic acid. As a polyunsaturated fatty acid, linoleic acid is prone to oxidation in the presence of oxygen. This oxidative reaction typically involves a free radical chain reaction. When antioxidants are added, they capture or react with free radicals. This causes neutralization of the progression of the oxidation reaction chain and a reduction in the degree of linoleic acid oxidation. The absorbance of xβ-CDs was almost constant over 7 days, comparable to the inhibitory activity of VC against linoleic acid (Fig. 2E). They inhibited lipid peroxidation in the linoleic acid model system. A decrease in absorbance at 500 nm indicates a greater degree of inhibition of lipid peroxidation (Memarpoor-Yazdi et al., 2012). Lecithin is among the key components of egg yolk. Lipid peroxidation of this component involves the formation of peroxidized lipids through the oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids in lecithin in the presence of oxygen. Antioxidants possibly inhibit the oxidative activity of lecithin by chelating with Fe2+. This chelation reduces the interaction between Fe2+ and lipid molecules, thereby preventing the progression of lipid peroxidation. Within the concentration range, the inhibitory effect of xβ-CDs against lipid peroxidation was closely related to their concentration, with higher concentrations yielding better inhibitory effects (Fig. 2F). Under the same conditions, xβ-CDs exhibited a higher rate of inhibition of lipid peroxidation than VC. At 2.0 mg/mL, the inhibition rate of xβ-CDs reached approximately 91.7 %, while that of VC was only approximately 43.4 %. Experimental results indicate that xβ-CDs have a strong ability to inhibit lipid peroxidation of egg yolk lecithin (Alamed et al., 2006). The capability of carbon dots to clear hydroxyl radicals, singlet oxygen, and hydrogen peroxide was evaluated through electron paramagnetic resonance testing. Using DMPO, TEMP, and CTPO as capture agents, the signals of the carbon dots were observed to determine their clearing abilities (Chakraborty et al., 2018; Wang, Xie, et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). Fig. 2G, H, and I indicate that in contrast with the control group, the xβ-CDs group displayed weak changes in the signal, indicating their strong capability to clear hydroxyl radicals, singlet oxygen, and H2O2. This demonstrates that carbon dots are crucial antioxidants and can operate through various antioxidant mechanisms.

Cell viability decreased with increasing H2O2 concentrations. For constructing the cell model, excessively high cell viability fails to induce oxidative stress, while excessively low cell viability results in irreversible cellular damage. When the cell viability is approximately 50 %, it provides a suitable condition to evaluate the effects of the samples on oxidative damage (Yue et al., 2022). At an H2O2 concentration of 1000.0 μmol/L for 4 h, the cell viability was 50.23 ± 0.63 %. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the cell viability in the model group was 50.905 ± 1.763 %. Compared to the model group, the cell viability of cells treated with xβ-CDs significantly increased, indicating that xβ-CDs provide protective effects against oxidative damage. When the concentrations of xβ-CDs were 200, 300, and 400 μmol/L, the cell viabilities were 85.934 ± 2.621 %, 86.931 ± 0.213 %, and 92.482 ± 2.886 %, respectively, demonstrating the most pronounced repair effects. Therefore, concentrations of 200.0, 300.0, and 400.0 μmol/L were selected for subsequent experiments. When the body is exposed to stimuli, it produces ROS, leading to oxidative imbalance and tissue damage (Pisoschi & Pop, 2015). Therefore, intracellular ROS levels are an important indicator of the extent of oxidative damage. The ROS levels in cells treated with xβ-CDs were measured using the DCFH-DA fluorescent probe, where the fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the ROS levels (Wolfe & Liu, 2007). As shown in the Fig. 3, compared to the control group, the model group exhibited an increased number of cells with fluorescence probe labeling and enhanced fluorescence intensity. After treatment with xβ-CDs, the fluorescence intensity showed a decrease to varying degrees. These results suggest that xβ-CDs can effectively protect Caco-2 cells from free radical damage.

As shown in the Fig. 3E, the SOD enzyme activity in cells treated with xβ-CDs was higher than that in the model group. When the xβ-CDs concentration was 400 μg/mL, the SOD enzyme activity was 24.36 ± 0.514 U/mg, which is 2.75 times that of the model group. This suggests that xβ-CDs can increase the content and activity of SOD in cells. MDA is a product of lipid peroxidation and its content can serve as an indicator of the extent of cellular damage and lipid peroxidation (Wu et al., 2018). As shown in the Fig. 3F, MDA levels in H2O2-treated cells were significantly higher than in the blank group. However, in the experimental group, the 400 μmol/L xβ-CDs treatment group showed the lowest MDA content, indicating that xβ-CDs can suppress lipid peroxidation to some extent, protecting cells from free radical damage. Catalase (CAT), present in cells and tissues, catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2, thereby preventing oxidative damage in the body (Abbasi-Oshaghi et al., 2018). As shown in the Fig. 3G, the CAT activity in the model group was the lowest, with a 31.34 % reduction compared to the blank group, indicating that H2O2 can reduce intracellular CAT activity. In contrast, CAT enzyme activity was significantly increased after xβ-CDs treatment, with the highest activity observed in the 400 μmol/L treatment group, reaching 35.5169 ± 2.1457 U/mg, which is 2.57 times that of the model group.

Determining the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) allows us to quantitatively compare the antibacterial capabilities of xβ-CDs against different bacterial strains. We used Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and other aquatic spoilage-causing bacteria for broad-spectrum antibacterial testing. Table 1 summarizes the MIC values of xβ-CDs against these strains. No bacteria grew under MIC (Fig. 4 A), and the results are consistent with those given in Table 1. The MIC values for E. coli, H. alvei, Serratia marcescens, S. aureus, and Aeromonas sobria were 1.0 mg/mL, whereas those for Pseudomonas fluorescens, P. fragi, and Listeria monocytogenes were 2.0 mg/mL. The study proposes that the negative charge displayed by carbon dots primarily stems from oxygen-containing functional groups like –OH, COOH, and C=O. These negative charges have the ability to cover the exterior of bacteria and induce toxicity on the bacteria cells due to biocontainment within the growth medium. Consequently, isolated bacteria cannot proliferate or consume nutrients. Therefore, disrupting their cell walls and membranes by using negatively charged carbon dots (Zhao, Liu, et al., 2023).

Green AO and red PI fluorescent staining were performed to assess the viability of H. alvei cells following xβ-CDs treatment (Fig. 4B). The green fluorescence indicates live H. alvei and the red fluorescence indicates dead H. alvei (Yang, Wang, et al., 2023). Fig. 4B shows that as the carbon dot concentration increased, the number of green fluorescent bacteria under the microscope also increased. This increase in fluorescence indicated a higher number of PI-stained cells, which suggested that carbon dots induce bacterial cell damage in a concentration-dependent manner. The 0.5 MIC treatment group had fewer dead cells than live cells, which indicated that the xβ-CDs concentration was insufficient to kill all bacteria. However, only a few live cells were observed in the overlapped images of xβ-CDs MIC-treated bacteria. In the images of xβ-CDs 2 MIC-treated H. alvei, almost no live cells were observed. However, only a red signal was noted from the dead cells. The results demonstrated that xβ-CDs effectively exerted antibacterial activity, with higher xβ-CDs concentrations resulting in a more pronounced effect. Moreover, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized for observe H. alvei morphology, which could be qualitatively and visually evaluated, providing direct insight into xβ-CDs-induced damage to H. alvei (Fig. 4C). The results revealed that in the control group, bacterial edges were clear, cell walls were intact, and rod-shaped structures were noted. By contrast, xβ-CDs-treated cell membranes were severely damaged, and the degree of bacterial destruction increased with higher xβ-CDs concentrations. At 0.5 MIC of xβ-CDs, the cell surface became rough and exhibited depressions. When the xβ-CDs concentration reached MIC, the depressions appeared on the bacterial surface, with a small amount of content leakage. At 2 MIC, the bacteria were severely damaged, exhibiting substantial shrinkage and extensive content leakage and ultimately leading to bacterial death (Du et al., 2023). These SEM results were consistent with those obtained from confocal laser scanning microscopy.

The growth curve is a graphical representation illustrating bacterial growth at different time points. These curves typically depict changes in bacterial quantity over time. In this study, the growth curve of xβ-CDs-treated H. alvei was determined. Fig. 5 A shows that are higher xβ-CDs concentrations, the H. alvei growth rate slowed down. In the control group, H. alvei grew rapidly and reached the highest OD600 nm value. After treatment with 2.0 mg/mL xβ-CDs, bacteria exhibited almost no growth, which indicated complete inhibition of bacterial growth. The group treated with 1.0 mg/mL xβ-CDs exhibited slow growth from 0 to 10 h and a significantly lower growth rate than the control group from 10 to 25 h. The growth rate became stable after 25 h. The growth patterns of the remaining treatment groups were comparable to H. alvei of the control group. The experimental results suggest that as the xβ-CDs concentration increased, the carbon dots had more opportunities to interact with bacteria and act on their cell walls, which effectively exerted antibacterial effects. Fluorescein diacetate (FDA) itself exhibits no fluorescence characteristics. Instead, when the FDA enters the cell, it is hydrolyzed by intracellular enzymes to fluorescein, which produces fluorescence. In an intact cell, fluorescence is observed within the cell. However, if the cell is damaged, fluorescein leaks out of the cell, thereby decreasing the fluorescence intensity inside the cell. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of FDA present inside bacterial cells can be measured to assess the permeability and integrity of bacterial cell membranes. Fig. 5B depicts the impact of varying xβ-CDs concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein inside H. alvei cells. According to the graph, the fluorescence intensity of the untreated group was the highest. After treatment with carbon dots, the fluorescence intensity decreased. The 2.0 mg/mL-treated group displayed the lowest fluorescence intensity, which indicated severe bacterial damage and increased cell membrane permeability. This result was consistent with the SEM results (Sun et al., 2021). Intracellular nonspecific esterases can cleave 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) to form DCFH. Under ROS conditions, DCFH undergoes further oxidation to produce fluorescent DCF. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of DCF represents the amount of ROS produced. As shown in Fig. 5C, xβ-CDs treatment significantly elevated intracellular ROS levels, and the ROS content increased as the xβ-CDs concentration. Therefore, xβ-CDs are inferred to generate ROS while damaging bacteria (Liu, Xu, et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022).

Proteins are pivotal players in the bacterial life cycle that influence cell transport, metabolism, growth, and reproduction. Fig. 5D illustrates the impact of varying xβ-CDs concentrations on the total protein content of H. alvei cells. The protein content in the control group increased with prolonged cultivation time, which was possibly related to bacterial growth and metabolism during culturing. With prolonged cultivation, the higher the xβ-CDs concentration, the less the decrease. Higher xβ-CDs concentrations result in the least amount of total protein. The findings indicate that xβ-CDs can rapidly and substantially damage H. alvei cells by increasing cell membrane permeability and causing leakage of intracellular macromolecular proteins (Cui et al., 2023). The activity of AKP is commonly measured to evaluate the degree of cell wall integrity disruption, as its release indicates cell wall damage (Wang et al., 2023). ATP is an intracellular energy currency that plays a critical role in cellular metabolism, respiration, and enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Na+/K+- ATPase play a pivotal role in maintaining the balance of transmembrane ion concentrations (Nong et al., 2024). As the xβ-CDs concentration increased, ATP and AKP enzyme levels were significantly higher in the control group (Fig. 5E, F). xβ-CDs addition effectively disrupted the cellular structure, with the highest concentration at 2 mg/mL increasing ATP and AKP inhibition. The results indicate that xβ-CDs disrupt the intact cellular structure, leading to cell apoptosis.

To investigate the inhibitory effect of xβ-CDs on H. alvei at the genetic level, qPCR analysis was performed on two related genes, ompA and murA . OMPs are essential components of the bacterial outer membrane, involved in substance transport and maintaining membrane stability (Witkowska et al., 2006). The murA gene encodes a key enzyme in the bacterial cell wall biosynthesis pathway. The MurA enzyme plays a crucial role in cell wall synthesis, which is fundamental for bacterial growth and division (Zhou et al., 2022). The xβ-CDs group suppressed the transcription of all tested genes (Fig. S15). The relative expression level of the control group is set to 1, while the relative expression levels of ompA and murA genes in the experimental group are 0.07748 and 0.05723, respectively. Based on the expression changes of the two genes, both ompA and murA genes show varying degrees of downregulation in the experimental group, indicating that the experimental treatment may have interfered with the formation of the bacterial cell membrane and cell wall, thereby affecting bacterial survival and adaptability.

Being a safe and effective antioxidant and antibacterial agent, the biocompatibility of synthesized xβ-CDs is imperative for food preservation. Therefore, we here selected mammalian cells for cytotoxicity testing and determined xβ-CDs-induced damage to these cells (Yang, Li, et al., 2023). Fig. 6 illustrates the safety test conducted on xβ-CDs. As the xβ-CDs concentration increased, the survival rate of the mammalian cells gradually decreased (Fig. 6A). At concentrations of 0–600 μg/mL, after 24 h of exposure to xβ-CDs, the cell survival rate remained above 85 %. This indicated the good biocompatibility of xβ-CDs and their potential use as food antioxidants and antibacterial agents. Additionally, the hemolysis rate is crucial for evaluating the biocompatibility of carbon dots. We selected rat erythrocytes for the hemolysis test (Shakiba-Marani & Ehtesabi, 2023). Fig. 6B presents the hemolysis capacity of xβ-CDs after they are cocultured with rat erythrocytes for 1 h. xβ-CDs had almost no effect on the erythrocytes, with a hemolysis rate of only 1.99 % noted when the xβ-CDs concentration reached 1 mg/mL. The highest hemolysis rate is still less than 5 %, which meets the safety standards of medical materials (Xiao et al., 2024). The experimental results indicated that xβ-CDs have good biocompatibility and can serve as safe and effective antioxidants and antibacterial agents. As shown in the Fig. 6C–H, the apoptosis rates of Caco-2 cells at different treatment concentrations were 2.96 %, 2.35 %, 2.19 %, 3.38 %, 3.32 %, and 3.40 %, respectively. The results demonstrate that xβ-CDs did not exhibit any adverse effects on the relative viability of Caco-2 cells, and they exhibit good biocompatibility at the tested concentrations, thereby confirming that these carbon dots possess favorable biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fresh-keeping effect of xβ-CDs on Rainbow trout fillets
ce:section.ce:para: Rainbow trout is a precious edible fish, and with spoilage, the edible value of this fish exhibits a direct decline, which is reflected through customers’ sensory experiences. The effects of protein degradation during spoilage are clearly evident as changes in color, alterations in odor, and transitions in tissue state. Fig. 7 A depicts the photographs of fish from different treatment groups over 12 days, showcasing a trend of decreasing sensory quality of Rainbow trout fillets with prolonged storage time. On day 0, all Rainbow trout fillets had a uniform color, clear texture, and no odor, which indicated freshness. On day 6, the Rainbow trout fillets in the control, 2-HP-β-CD, xylitol, and sodium dehydroacetate groups began to spoil, with the softening of fish flesh, color darkening, a rotting fishy smell, and the emergence of white spots. Conversely, the xβ-CDs group effectively deferred the fish's decay process. By day 12, the fish color in the xβ-CDs group had just started to darken, whereas the other groups exhibited complete deterioration. This phenomenon may be attributable to the antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of xβ-CDs, which decelerate the decay of Rainbow trout and effectively extend its shelf life by 3 days.

The meat quality characteristics are determined by the microscopic state of the Rainbow trout muscle tissue. Moreover, they were a visual representation of fish freshness. At the start of the study, the muscle tissue fibers of Rainbow trout were firmly bound, and structurally smooth. By day 6, the muscle tissue fibers in the control, 2-HP-β-CD, xylitol, and sodium dehydroacetate groups gradually became break, loosen, and disordered. By day 12, the muscle tissue fibers in the control, 2-HP-β-CD, xylitol, and sodium dehydroacetate groups became a chaotic state and a near-complete structural failure, which indicated a complete rotten. On the contrary, the xβ-CDs group still maintained some intact fibers, but also showed loose arrangement. This was similar to the above results for TVC, TVB-N, and TBA (Fig. 7B).

Seafood is rich in a variety of nutrients. These are considered ideal nutrients for microbial growth and reproduction. Consequently, the presence of these nutrients accelerates microbial proliferation, leading to seafood spoilage and deterioration. Therefore, Rainbow trout freshness was assessed on the basis of microbial indicators. TVC values exceeding 6 lg CFU/g indicated that Rainbow trout had deteriorated and was no longer edible (Lin et al., 2022). According to Fig. 7C, the TVC values of fresh Rainbow trout was 1.73 ± 0.067 lg CFU/g. The TVC values of the control, 2-HP-β-CD, xylitol, sodium dehydroacetate, and xβ-CDs groups increased as the storage time. However, the growth rate of the TVC values was relatively lower in the xβ-CDs group, which indicated that xβ-CDs exerted a good antimicrobial effect on the microbiota in Rainbow trout. On the 7th day, the TVC values of the control, 2-HP-β-CD, and xylitol groups exceeded the limit, thereby reaching the expiration date. On the 10th day, the TVC values of the sodium dehydroacetate group exceeded the limit, thus reaching the expiration date. The xβ-CDs group reached its limit of TVC values on the 12th day. TVB-N represents basic nitrogenous compounds, including ammonia and amines, and are produced through protein degradation during refrigeration of aquatic products. They are a crucial indicator of the freshness of seafood products (Chen et al., 2022). When the TVB-N concentration is higher than 30 mg/100 g, the fish will decay (Cui et al., 2023). Throughout storage, the TVB-N values exhibited an overall increasing trend with prolonged storage time (Fig. 7D). By day 6, the TVB-N values of the control, 2-HP-β-CD, and xylitol groups surpassed the permissible threshold, indicating that the fish meat was completely spoiled and was not edible. The sodium dehydroacetate group surpassed the permissible threshold of the TVB-N value on the 9th day, while the xβ-CDs group surpassed the permissible threshold on the 12th day. Throughout storage, the TVB-N value was relatively lower in the xβ-CDs group. xβ-CDs addition further augmented antimicrobial activity, and fish oxidation was also accelerated because of a rise in TVB-N levels. Therefore, the xβ-CDs treatment group exhibited the lowest TVB-N content, possibly because of its combined antimicrobial and antioxidant effects.

Because lipids in fish meat are susceptible to oxidation, the nutritional value decreases, leading to fish spoilage. Malondialdehyde (MDA) produced through unsaturated fatty acid decomposition during storage can be utilized to assess the extent of lipid oxidation. After day 6, the TBA values sharply increased in all groups (Fig. 7E). Samples from all the control, 2-HP-β-CD, and xylitol groups exhibited high lipid oxidation. The sodium dehydroacetate group exhibited some inhibition compared with the aforementioned three groups. The xβ-CDs group exhibited a significantly lower trend of increase in TBA values during the initial storage period than the other four groups, with a lower growth rate noted in the xβ-CDs group than in the other groups. This indicates that the added xβ-CDs inhibited the auto-oxidation and hydrolysis of Rainbow trout.

Muscle proteins, particularly myofibrillar proteins (MPs), are susceptible to the effects of ROS during storage, which results in protein oxidation via free radicals. Among the amino acid residues of proteins, cysteine residues are the most prone to oxidation. The extent of cysteine oxidation in proteins is reflected as changes in the total sulfhydryl content. The total sulfhydryl content of fresh Rainbow trout fillets was 38.3 nmol/mg (Fig. 8 A). Over the storage period, the total sulfhydryl content in the control, 2-HP-β-CD, xylitol, and sodium dehydroacetate groups gradually decreased. The xβ-CDs group exhibited a significantly lower decline than the other four groups. xβ-CDs addition caused a reduction in the total sulfhydryl content. This phenomenon may have occurred because hydrogen atoms from the total sulfhydryl groups of cysteine were preferentially extracted by the electrophilic hydroxyl groups, which further reduced total sulfhydryl oxidation (Wang, Zhao, et al., 2024). Similar to total thiols, the carbonyl content is a crucial indicator of protein oxidation during the storage of fresh Rainbow trout fillets. The carbonyl content gradually increased with the storage time (Fig. 8B). By day 12, the carbonyl content in the xβ-CDs group had only increased to 2.443 mmol/mg, which was relatively lower compared to the other groups. Additionally, surface hydrophobicity can be used to detect changes in the distribution of hydrophobic groups on the protein surface, thereby reflecting the degree of protein denaturation and hydrolysis. The oxidation of amino acids in proteins and the unfolding of peptide chains trigger the exposure of hydrophobic groups, provoking a rise in surface hydrophobicity (Wang, Zhao, et al., 2024). Fig. 8C displays variations in the binding amount of MP with BPB. The amount of MP that binds with BPB was relatively lower in the xβ-CDs group compared to the other groups, which indicated that xβ-CDs effectively delayed protein denaturation, thereby reducing the exposure of hydrophobic groups on the surface of MP. The activity of Ca2+/Mg2+- ATPase, located at the myosin head, is considered an indicator of MP integrity (Wu et al., 2014). By assessing Ca2+/Mg2+- ATPase activity, MP decomposition can be determined. The rate of decline in Ca2+/Mg2+- ATPase activity in the control group was the fastest, similar to that in the 2-HP-β-CD and xylitol groups (Fig. 8D). By contrast, the rate of decline in Ca2+/Mg2+- ATPase activity significantly decelerated in the xβ-CDs group. This suggests that xβ-CDs effectively reduce protein oxidative decomposition.

These data indicate that xβ-CDs effectively decelerated microbial growth, and lipid and protein oxidation rates in Rainbow trout, which is attributable to their potent antimicrobial and antioxidant properties.

In this study, bifunctional xβ-CDs with super antioxidant and high antibacterial abilities were prepared through the one-pot hydrothermal method by using xylitol and 2-HP-β-CD as precursors. The carbon dots exhibited good water solubility and biocompatibility. They primarily exerted excellent antioxidant effects through –OH groups, thereby significantly inhibiting DPPH, ABTS, ·OH radicals, and KMnO4. Moreover, xβ-CDs displayed high inhibitory activity against peroxidation of the lipids linoleic acid and egg yolk lecithin. They also exerted strong scavenging effects on ROS associated with cellular oxidative stress. Similarly, they exhibited potent antibacterial effects, thus exerting broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against various bacteria causing spoilage of aquatic products. The results against H. alvei indicated that xβ-CDs disrupt the integrity of bacterial cell walls and membranes; reduce total protein, AKP, and ATP enzyme contents; and elevate intracellular ROS levels, which leads to bacterial death. Importantly, xβ-CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity to mammalian cells. The storage experiments confirmed that xβ-CDs successfully prolonged the storage duration of Rainbow trout fillets by 3 days. In conclusion, our hypothesis has been strongly validated by the results of this study. Both xylitol and 2-HP-β-CD play pivotal roles in the formation and functionality of the carbon dots. Under hydrothermal conditions, xylitol undergoes carbonization, facilitating the formation of carbon dots while simultaneously endowing them with reactive surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, essential for antioxidant activity. On the other hand, 2-HP-β-CD enhances the stability and dispersion of the carbon dots in aqueous environments due to its hydrophilic 2-hydroxypropyl groups. This modification increases the surface reactivity of the carbon dots, particularly enhancing their ability to interact with free radicals and bacterial cells, thus significantly boosting their antioxidant properties, and transforming the no antibacterial precursor into an antibacterial material. These results suggested that the dual functionality of xβ-CDs, with excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, present a novel strategy for preventing bacterial infections, protein oxidation, and lipid oxidation in food preservation.
